core_takeaway: Agent_1's first position looks advantaged because the negotiation quickly made Apple part of the protected baseline, while Agent_5's path to a slightly better final item was treated as a consent-gated minor refinement and then abandoned. The advantage was not unlimited: Agent_1's richer Round 2 grab failed once it threatened Jewel.
why_or_counterexample: The first position benefited from an early stable allocation that gave Agent_1 its top item and became the eventual consensus. Agent_5, despite valuing Jewel and Quill more than Stone, accepted the baseline because Jewel and Quill were protected for Agents 3 and 2, and the only direct improvement for Agent_5 from Stone to Pencil required mutual consent that was not achieved.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Public discussion anchored Apple with Agent_1, Quill with Agent_2, and Jewel with Agent_3; later rounds repeatedly limited changes to a consent-based Pencil/Stone swap.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Present; private plans consistently protected Quill/Jewel, kept Apple stable, and treated Pencil/Stone as the safe but consent-gated lever.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Round 1 and Round 3 proposals converged on the same baseline; the Round 2 Agent_1 deviation was rejected 1-4.
phase_notes.private_voting: Present; Agent_4 initially held out for a Pencil/Stone upgrade, Agent_1 later held out for extra utility, and Round 3 all agents accepted the baseline.
phase_notes.reflection: Present; reflections identify top-item protection and mutual consent for Pencil/Stone as the central blockers, making the baseline the default consensus path.

mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 named Apple as its top priority before others spoke, and the shared baseline quickly framed Apple-to-Agent_1 as compatible with everyone else's protected top items. That baseline carried through to the final consensus and produced Agent_1's higher discounted utility.

evidence.quote: honoring Agent_1’s priority to keep Apple

mechanisms.theme: Protected top items crowded out Agent_5
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_5 valued Jewel more than Stone, but the public and private consensus treated Jewel as Agent_3's protected item and Quill as Agent_2's protected item. That left Agent_5 in the non-top-item pool while Agent_1 retained Apple.

evidence.quote: Jewel stays with me in Round 2.
keeping Quill with Agent_2 and Jewel with Agent_3
gives me Stone (17) for a discounted utility of 13.77

mechanisms.theme: Consent gate froze the last-position upgrade
mechanisms.explanation: The one simple improvement for Agent_5 was Stone-to-Pencil, but it was framed as requiring explicit mutual consent from Agents 4 and 5. Agent_5 itself rejected that refinement in voting because the required consensus was unlikely, so the group reverted to the baseline that favored Agent_1 over Agent_5.

evidence.quote: Pencil↔Stone would give me Pencil (18) instead of Stone (17)
I cannot support a change that relies on likely-unmet consensus
Pencil↔Stone swap requires mutual consent from 4 and 5; since not achieved

mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 could not simply use first position to claim more items. Its Round 2 proposal gave itself Apple plus Stone and moved Jewel away from Agent_3, and the private votes rejected that as a violation of the top-item protection norm.

evidence.quote: Jewel must stay with Agent_3 in Round 2.

mechanisms.theme: Final convergence copied the early baseline
mechanisms.explanation: By Round 3, every formal proposal used the same baseline allocation: Agent_1 kept Apple and Agent_5 kept Stone. Unanimity arrived only after that conservative allocation was accepted as risk-minimizing.

evidence.quote: avoid negotiation risk around top items
